source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
title: Do not create access keys during initial setup for IAM users with a console password

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
input_row.title: Do not create access keys during initial setup for IAM users with a console password
input_row.description: AWS console defaults to no check boxes selected when creating a new IAM user. When creating the IAM User credentials you have to determine what type of access they require. Programmatic access: The IAM user might need to make API calls, use the AWS CLI, or use the Tools for Windows PowerShell. In that case, create an access key (access key ID and a secret access key) for that user. AWS Management Console access: If the user needs to access the AWS Management Console, create a password for the user.
input_row.rationale: Requiring the additional steps to be taken by the user for programmatic access after their profile has been created will provide a stronger indication of intent that access keys are [a] necessary for their work and [b] that once the access key is established on an account, the keys may be in use somewhere in the organization. Note : Even if it is known the user will need access keys, require them to create the keys themselves or put in a support ticket to have them created as a separate step from user creation.
input_row.audit: Perform the following steps to determine if unused access keys were created upon user creation: From Console: 1. Login to the AWS Management Console 2. Click Services 3. Click IAM 4. Click on a User where column Password age and Access key age is not set to None 5. Click on Security credentials Tab 6. Compare the user Creation time to the Access Key Created date. 7. For any that match, the key was created during initial user setup. • Keys that were created at the same time as the user profile and do not have a last used date should be deleted. Refer to the remediation below. From Command Line: 1. Run the following command (OSX/Linux/UNIX) to generate a list of all IAM users along with their access keys utilization: aws iam generate-credential-report aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' --output text | base64 -d | cut -d, -f1,4,9,11,14,16 2. The output of this command will produce a table similar to the following: user,password_enabled,access_key_1_active,access_key_1_last_used_date,access_ key_2_active,access_key_2_last_used_date elise,false,true,2015-04-16T15:14:00+00:00,false,N/A brandon,true,true,N/A,false,N/A rakesh,false,false,N/A,false,N/A helene,false,true,2015-11-18T17:47:00+00:00,false,N/A paras,true,true,2016-08-28T12:04:00+00:00,true,2016-03-04T10:11:00+00:00 anitha,true,true,2016-06-08T11:43:00+00:00,true,N/A 3. For any user having password_enabled set to true AND access_key_last_used_date set to N/A refer to the remediation below.
input_row.remediation: Perform the following to delete access keys that do not pass the audit: From Console: 1. Login to the AWS Management Console: 2. Click Services 3. Click IAM 4. Click on Users 5. Click on Security Credentials 6. As an Administrator • Click on the X (Delete) for keys that were created at the same time as the user profile but have not been used. 7. As an IAM User • Click on the X (Delete) for keys that were created at the same time as the user profile but have not been used.  From Command Line: aws iam delete-access-key --access-key-id <access-key-id-listed> --user-name <users-name> References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/iam/delete-access-key.html 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_users_create.html Additional Information: Credential report does not appear to contain "Key Creation Date"
input_row.references: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/iam/delete-access-key.html 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/id_users_create.html Additional Information: Credential report does not appear to contain "Key Creation Date"

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control requires verification that access keys were not created for IAM users during their initial setup when they have a console password. This check can be accomplished using AWS APIs and CLI tools to generate and analyze an IAM credential report. The AWS IAM service provides sufficient API and CLI support to automate this verification process.

**PROGRAM NAME:** aws_iam_user_initial_setup_access_key_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the AWS CLI command `aws iam generate-credential-report` to create a credential report.
- Use the AWS CLI command `aws iam get-credential-report` to retrieve the generated credential report in a human-readable format.
- Parse the report to check for IAM users who have `password_enabled` set to `true` and whose access keys do not have a `last_used_date` (indicating they are unused).
- Ensure that there are no access keys that were created at the same time as the user profile and do not have a last used date.
- Pass Criteria: No IAM users should have access keys that were created at the same time as the user with no usage recorded.
- Validate results across all IAM users to ensure full compliance with the control requirements.
step2_review:
### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification as automated is generally correct because the AWS CLI and APIs can be used to generate and analyze IAM credential reports. However, the current automation approach does not fully address the control's requirement to ensure no access keys are created during the initial setup for users with console passwords.
- **AWS APIs/boto3/CLI Capability:** The use of `aws iam generate-credential-report` and `aws iam get-credential-report` is valid for gathering IAM user credential data. However, the current method does not explicitly check if access keys were created during the initial setup, which requires correlating the creation time of the user and the access keys.

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: 7

- **Automation Details:** The use of `aws iam generate-credential-report` and `aws iam get-credential-report` is technically correct for retrieving credential data. However, the logic to determine if access keys were created during the initial setup is not explicitly outlined.
- **Boto3 Methods or CLI Commands:** The CLI commands mentioned are valid, but the logic for checking the creation time of the access keys relative to the user creation time is missing.
- **Program Name:** The program name `aws_iam_user_initial_setup_access_key_check` is descriptive but could be more precise by including terms like `creation_time_check` to clarify that the creation time is being verified.

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS:

- **Missing Considerations:** The current approach does not explicitly check the creation time of access keys relative to the user creation time. This can be improved by using the `list_users` and `list_access_keys` boto3 methods to retrieve the creation dates and compare them.
- **Alternative AWS API Methods:** Use the `boto3` library to fetch user creation dates and access key creation dates. This can be done using:
  ```python
  import boto3

  iam_client = boto3.client('iam')
  users = iam_client.list_users()['Users']
  
  for user in users:
      user_name = user['UserName']
      user_creation_date = user['CreateDate']
      
      access_keys = iam_client.list_access_keys(UserName=user_name)['AccessKeyMetadata']
      for key in access_keys:
          if key['CreateDate'] == user_creation_date:
              print(f"User {user_name} has an access key created at the same time as the user.")
  ```
- **Better Program Name Suggestion:** `aws_iam_user_initial_setup_key_creation_time_check`

### 4. CONFIDENCE: MEDIUM

- The assessment correctly identifies the use of AWS CLI for automation, but the logic for checking the creation time of access keys is incomplete. The confidence is medium due to the missing correlation logic.

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: Modify

- **Modify:** Enhance the automation script to include logic that checks the creation time of access keys relative to the user creation time. This will ensure compliance with the control requirement that no access keys are created during the initial setup for users with console passwords. Use the `boto3` library to fetch and compare creation dates for a more comprehensive check.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions agree that this control can be automated using AWS APIs. The AWS IAM credential report provides both user creation time and access key creation time, allowing for programmatic comparison to determine if access keys were created during initial user setup for users with console passwords. The only refinement needed is to ensure the automation explicitly compares these timestamps, which is fully supported by the data available in the credential report.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
aws_iam_user_access_key_creation_time_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- AWS Resource/Service: IAM Users
- API/CLI Method: 
  - `aws iam generate-credential-report`
  - `aws iam get-credential-report`
- Properties to Check:
  - `user_creation_time`
  - `access_key_1_active`, `access_key_1_last_rotated`, `access_key_1_last_used_date`
  - `access_key_2_active`, `access_key_2_last_rotated`, `access_key_2_last_used_date`
  - `password_enabled`
- Pass Criteria:
  - For any IAM user with `password_enabled` = `true`, there are no access keys (`access_key_1_active` or `access_key_2_active`) where the creation time (`access_key_1_last_rotated` or `access_key_2_last_rotated`) matches the `user_creation_time` and the key has not been used (`access_key_1_last_used_date` or `access_key_2_last_used_date` is `N/A`).
- Fail Criteria:
  - Any IAM user with `password_enabled` = `true` has an access key created at the same time as the user (`access key last rotated` = `user creation time`) and the key has not been used (`last used date` is `N/A`).

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
The second opinion noted the need for explicit timestamp comparison. This is addressed in the automation details, ensuring the check is precise and fully automated using available AWS APIs.

**SOURCE:** CIS AMAZON WEB SERVICES FOUNDATIONS BENCHMARK V5.0.0
